content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

September 16, 2010

John Bezmalinovic
Granville Homes, Inc.
1396 West Herndon Avenue, Suite 101
Fresno, California 93711

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-10-137

Dear Mr. Bezmalinovic:

This letter is in response to your request for advice, on behalf of Darius Assemi, regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090.

QUESTION

As a member of the California Transportation Commission (the "CTC"), may Commissioner Darius Assemi make, participate in making, or otherwise use his official position to influence the CTC's decisions relating to the construction of a highway interchange in Fresno County, if he has investments in business entities that own real property in the county?

CONCLUSION

Commissioner Assemi may not make, participate in making or use his official position to influence the CTC's decisions. It is reasonably foreseeable that the CTC's decisions will have a material financial effect on his real property because the interchange would increase access to the property and would, therefore, increase the value of the property and its income producing potential.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-10-137
Page No. 2

FACTS

Darius Assemi serves as a commissioner of the CTC. The CTC is responsible for:

• Adopting a five-year State Transportation Improvement Program ("STIP") and approving the biennial four-year State Highway Operation and Protection Program ("SHOPP").
• Adopting a biennial five-year fund estimate of state and federal funds expected to be available for the STIP and SHOPP.
• Allocating state funds for capital projects, consistent with the STIP and SHOPP.
• Allocating state funds for capital grants from the Aeronautics Account and from the Environmental Enhancement and Mitigation Program Fund.
• Adopting guidelines for the development of the STIP and for the development of regional transportation plans.
• Advising and assisting the Secretary of Business, Transportation and Housing Agency and the Legislature in formulating and evaluating state policies and plans for transportation programs in the state.
• Adopting and submitting to the Legislature an annual report summarizing major policies and decisions adopted by the CTC and identifying timely and relevant transportation issues, including a discussion of any significant upcoming transportation issues anticipated to be of concern to the public and the legislature.

It is anticipated that the CTC will be asked to approve a new connection to Highway 99 in the County of Fresno consisting of a standard partial cloverleaf interchange at the intersection of Veterans Boulevard and Highway 99. Veterans Boulevard is a six-lane boulevard beginning at the intersection of Grantland and Gettysburg Avenues, running northeasterly and intersecting Highway 99 near the Bulkard Avenue alignment, then continuing northeasterly to connect with Herndon Avenue west of Polk Avenue. The alignment has been part of a component of Fresno's General Plan since 1984. Construction of the interchange was approved by the Fresno City Council and the Fresno County Board of Supervisors in 1996. A Project Study Report was completed and approved in June 2009. Preparation of Project Approval/Environmental Documents is underway. This is the phase during which it is expected that the CTC will be asked to approve the proposed interchange.

Commissioner Assemi owns a 10 percent or greater interest worth $2,000 or more in seven business entities that own approximately 308 acres of real property located in the area west of Highway 99:

La Ventana Property: Grantland Avenue, LLC owns approximately 76 residential lots currently being marketed as part of the "La Ventana community." GVM Development, LLC owns undeveloped property that is part of an approved vesting tentative map that has been approved, covers approximately 9.5 acres of property and is zoned and will be developed as multi-family housing or condominiums. The La Ventana property is located approximately 600 feet from the proposed interchange.

File No. A-10-137
Page No. 3

Garfield Avenue Property: GVM Development, LLC and Granville at Barstow, Inc. own
approximately 32.73 acres of property located at the northeast corner of Garfield Avenue and
Barstow Avenue. A vesting tentative map for this tract has been approved subdividing this
property as single-family lots. Plans are contemplated for the first final map of this tract. This
property is located approximately 4,600 feet from the proposed intersection.

Westlake Property: ACAP Holdings, LLC, GVM Development, LLC, GVM Investments,
LLC, West Fresno Holdings, LLC and Granville at Westlake, Inc. own 238.5 acres located within
the County of Fresno. This property is within the City of Fresno's Sphere of Influence and is
expected to be become part of the city if the Local Agency Formation Commission ("LAFCO")
approves the annexation of this property to the City of Fresno. The Westlake property is located
approximately 1.7 miles from the proposed interchange.

You have stated that it is presently not practical to determine whether a decision by the
CTC to approve the interchange will have any financial effect on these business entities. You
have also stated that the CTC's decision will not affect the expenses of any of the relevant
businesses in the amount $5,000 or more and it is speculative to say whether the decision will
result in an increase/decrease of $20,000 or more in the gross revenues, or the assets/liabilities,
of the involved business entities. You have asked whether Commissioner Assemi may make,
participate in making, or influence the CTC's decision under the general conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Act and, if not, whether the public generally exception applies.

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will "perform their
duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the
financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).) Section 87100
prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial
interest.

The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether an
official has a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Regulation 18700(b).) The general rule,
however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official makes a governmental
decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of his or her
financial interests.

Steps 1 and 2. Is Commissioner Assemi a "public official" within the meaning of Section
87100? Will Commissioner Assemi be making, participating in making or influencing a
governmental decision?

As a CTC Commissioner, Commissioner Assemi is a public official and that by
participating in the CTC's vote on approval of the interchange, he will be making or participating
in making a governmental decision.

File No. A-10-137
Page No. 4


Step 3. What are Commissioner Assemi's economic interests?

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts of interest arising from certain enumerated economic interests. These economic interests are described in Section 87103 and Regulations 18703-18703.5, inclusive:

• A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a); Regulation 18703.1(a).)

• A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d); Regulation 18703.1(b).)

• A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b); Regulation 18703.2.)

• An official has an economic interest in any source of income, including promised income, totaling $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3.)

• A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts total $420 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(e); Regulation 18703.4.)

• A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family. This is commonly referred to as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5.)

BUSINESS ENTITIES

Commissioner Assemi has an economic interest in Grantland Avenue, LLC GVM Development, LLC, Grantville at Barstow, Inc., ACAP Holdings, LLC, GVM Investments, LLC, West Fresno Holdings, LLC and Granville at Westlake, Inc. as business entities on two bases. First, Commissioner Assemi has an investment of $2,000 or more in each of these business entities. Second, he holds a management position with each of the business entities.

REAL PROPERTY

Commissioner Assemi has an indirect economic interest in the three properties owned by the business entities listed above because he owns a 10% or greater interest in each of the business entities and his investment is worth $2,000 or more. (Section 87103.)

File No. A-10-137
Page No. 5


PERSONAL FINANCES

A public official always has an economic interest in his or her personal finances.
(Regulation 18703.5.) However, a financial effect on the gross revenues, expenses, or value of
assets/liabilities of a business entity in which a public official has a direct or indirect investment
interest, and a financial effect on the value of real property owned directly or indirectly by the
public official, are not considered a separate financial effect on the official's personal finances
and are not analyzed separately under the "personal financial effects" rule. (Regulation
18705.5(a).) Accordingly, the personal financial effects rule does not to apply to Commissioner
Assemi's circumstances and we will not discuss it further.

Step 4. Will Commissioner Assemi's economic interests be directly or indirectly involved
in decisions he will make, participate in making or influence as a public official?

BUSINESS ENTITIES

Under Regulation 18704.1(a) a person, including business entities and sources of income,
is directly involved in a decision before an official's agency when that person, either directly or
by an agent:

"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by
filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;

"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding
concerning the decision before the official or the official's agency.
A person is the subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license,
permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject
person."

You have stated that the business entities in which Commissioner Assemi has an economic
interest will neither initiate nor be a party in or the subject of the CTC's proceedings in which a
governmental decision will be made regarding the proposed interchange. Therefore, the business
entities are not directly involved in the decision. If a public official's economic interest is not
directly involved in a governmental decision, it is indirectly involved. (Regulation 18704).

REAL PROPERTY

Under Section 18704.2(a), real property is directly involved in a governmental decision
if:

"(1) The real property in which the official has an interest, or any
part of that real property, is located within 500 feet of the

File No. A-10-137
Page No. 6

boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the property which is
the subject of the governmental decision. For purposes of
subdivision (a)(5), real property is located within 500 feet of the
boundaries (or proposed boundaries) of the real property which is
the subject of the governmental decision if any part of the real
property is within 500 feet of the boundaries (or proposed
boundaries) of the redevelopment project area.

"(2) The governmental decision involves the zoning or rezoning,
annexation or deannexation, sale, purchase, or lease, or inclusion
in or exclusion from any city, county, district or other local
governmental subdivision, of the real property in which the official
has an interest or a similar decision affecting the real property. For
purposes of this subdivision, the terms 'zoning' and 'rezoning'
shall refer to the act of establishing or changing the zoning or land
use designation on the real property in which the official has an
interest.

"(3) The governmental decision involves the issuance, denial or
revocation of a license, permit or other land use entitlement
authorizing a specific use or uses of the real property in which the
official has an interest.

"(4) The governmental decision involves the imposition, repeal or
modification of any taxes or fees assessed or imposed on the real
property in which the official has an interest.

"(5) The governmental decision is to designate the survey area, to
select the project area, to adopt the preliminary plan, to form a
project area committee, to certify the environmental document, to
adopt the redevelopment plan, to add territory to the
redevelopment area, or to rescind or amend any of the above
decisions; and real property in which the official has an interest, or
any part of it is located within the boundaries (or the proposed
boundaries) of the redevelopment area.

"(6) The decision involves construction of, or improvements to,
streets, water, sewer, storm drainage or similar facilities, and the
real property in which the official has an interest will receive new
or improved services."

Because the real property owned by the business entities in which Commissioner Assemi has an
economic interest does not fall within any of the foregoing categories, it is deemed to be
indirectly involved.

File No. A-10-137
Page No. 7


Step 5. What is the applicable materiality standard?

A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
governmental decision on a public official's economic interest is material. (Regulation
18700(a).)

BUSINESS ENTITIES

The applicable materiality standard for governmental decisions involving business
entities is found in Regulation 18705.1. Subdivision (c) sets forth materiality standards for
economic interests in business entities that are indirectly involved in a governmental decision,
including those that are sources of income. (See Regulation 18705.3(b)(1). For relatively small
businesses, the financial effect of a governmental decision on the business entity is material if it
is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will increase/decrease the business
entity's annual gross revenues by $20,000 or more, result in the business entity
incurring/avoiding additional expenses or reducing/eliminating existing expenses by $5,000 or
more in a fiscal year or increase/decrease the value of the business entity's assets/liabilities by
$20,000 or more. (Regulation 18705.1(c)(4).) Based on the size of the business entities, as stated
in your letter, this is the applicable materiality standard.

REAL PROPERTY

The financial effect of a governmental decision on real property that is indirectly
involved in the governmental decision is presumed not to be material. This presumption may be
rebutted by proof that there are specific circumstances regarding the governmental decision, its
financial effect, and the nature of the real property which make it reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect on the real property in which the public official has
an interest. Examples of such specific circumstances include the development potential or
income producing potential of the real property, the use of the real property and the character of
the neighborhood, including substantial effects on traffic, view, privacy, intensity of use, noise
levels and air emissions. (Regulation 18705.2(b)(1).)

Step 6. Is it reasonably foreseeable that the financial effect of the Commission's decisions
on Commissioner Assemi's economic interests will meet the applicable materiality
standard?

An effect is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if the effect is "substantially
likely." (Regulation 18706; In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) 198.),198.) A financial
effect need not be a certainty to be considered reasonably foreseeable. On the other hand, if an
effect is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable.

BUSINESS ENTITIES:

File No. A-10-137
Page No. 8

You have indicated that the CTC's decision will not affect any of the expenses of the
relevant businesses for a fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 or more. You have also stated that
"it is not practical to determine whether the proposed decision will have any financial effect on
those business entities and even more difficult to quantify that effect, if any." The decision on the
gross revenues/liabilities of the businesses would be speculative. Under these circumstances,
there is a possibility that the CTC's decision may meet the materiality thresholds set forth in
Regulation 18705.1(c). However, as stated above, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable. The Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providing advice. (In
re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 72.) The determination of whether it is reasonably foreseeable
that the CTC's decision will affect the gross revenues of the business entities by $20,000 or
more, or the assets/liabilities by $5,000 or more, is necessarily a factual issue that is ultimately
for Commissioner Assemi to decide.

REAL PROPERTY:

La Ventana Property: The La Ventana property is located approximately 600 feet from
the proposed interchange and has been subdivided, improved and nearly all lots have been sold.
The lone exceptions are a proposed multi-family parcel and a proposed commercial parcel.
These parcels have received the necessary zoning and other basic entitlements.

Regulation 18705.2(b)(1) provides that the presumption of non-materiality may be
rebutted by proof that there are specific circumstances regarding the governmental decision, its
financial effect, and the nature of the real property that make it reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect on the real property. The location of the La
Ventana property 600 feet from the proposed interchange, and the nature of the parcel that is
zoned for commercial use are "specific circumstances" that rebut the presumption. Generally, it
can be expected that increased access to a commercial property by its proximity to a freeway
interchange would enhance the value of the property or increase its income-producing potential.
Under these circumstances, Commissioner Assemi may not make, participate in making, or
attempt to influence governmental decisions regarding the proposed interchange.

Garfield Avenue Property: This property is located approximately 4,600 feet from the
proposed interchange. The Garfield Avenue property has secured zoning approvals and is the
subject of a vesting tentative map. The vesting tentative map establishes the conditions of
approval for each further phase of the subdivision. Because we have determined that
Commissioner Assemi may not participate in the governmental decision regarding the proposed
interchange, we need not reach the question of whether the presumption of non-materiality is
rebutted with respect to the Garfield Avenue property.

Westlake Property: The Westlake property is located approximately 1.7 miles from the
proposed interchange. A tentative map covering this property has been submitted and the
environmental document is being prepared. It is expected that the property will be annexed into
the City of Fresno. It is anticipated the proposed interchange will help relieve projected traffic
congestion in the immediate area, including the Westlake Property. It is also anticipated that
residents as far west as the town of Kerman, located approximately 15 miles from the proposed
interchange, will benefit from improved access to the greater Fresno community. Because we

File No. A-10-137
Page No. 9

have determined that Commissioner Assemi may not participate in the governmental decision
regarding the proposed interchange, we need not reach the question of whether the presumption
of non-materiality is rebutted with respect to the Westlake property.²

Step 7: The "public generally" exception.

Even if a material financial effect on a public official's economic interest is reasonably
foreseeable, he or she still may not be disqualified if the financial effect of the governmental
decision on the public official's economic interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the
public generally. (Section 87103; Regulations 18700(b)(7) and 18707(a).) Because
Commissioner Assemi has substantial business interests and property holdings, it is extremely
unlikely that his interests will be affected in substantially the same manner as the public
generally.

Step 8: The "legally required participation" exception.

You have not presented any facts indicating that this exception is applicable to
Commissioner Assemi's situation.

If you have other questions on this matter please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

By: Valentina Joyce
Counsel, Legal Division

VJ:jgl

² In the event that Commissioner Assemi no longer has an economic interest in the La Ventana property at
the time CTC considers approval of the interchange, we recommend that you contact the Commission again. There
is a concern that his interest in the Westlake property may give rise to a disqualifying conflict of interest.

sections.analysis:
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will "perform their
duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the
financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).) Section 87100
prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial
interest.
The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether an
official has a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Regulation 18700(b).) The general rule,
however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official makes a governmental
decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or *or" of his or her
financial interests.
Steps 1 and 2. Is Commissioner Assemi a "public official" within the meaning of Section
87100? Will Commissioner Assemi be making, participating in making or influencing a
governmental decision?
As a CTC Commissioner, Commissioner Assemi is a public official and that by
participating in the CTC's vote on approval of the interchange, he will be making or participating
in making a governmental decision.
Step 3. What are Commissioner Assemi's economic interests?
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts of interest arising
from certain enumerated economic interests. These economic interests are described in Section
87103 and Regulations 18703- 18703.5, inclusive:
o A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he
or she has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a); Regulation
18703.1(a).)
o A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or
she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management.
(Section 87 103(d); Regulation 18703. 1(b).)
o I public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she
has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b); Regularion L8703.2.)
o An official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, totaling $500 or more within l2 months prior to the decision. (Section

o A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her
if the gifts total $420 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(e);
Regulation 18703.4.)

income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family. This is commonly

A person is the subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the

person."
You have stated that the business entities in which Commissioner Assemi has an economic

in or exclusion from any city, county, district or other local
governmental subdivision, of the real property in which the official
has an interest or a similar decision affecting the real property. For
purposes of this subdivision, the terms 'zoning' and .rezoning'
shall refer to the act of establishing or changing the zoning or land
use designation on the real property in which the official has an
interest.
"(3) The govemmental decision involves the issuance, denial or
revocation of a license, permit or other land use entitlement
authorizing a specific use or uses of the real property in which the
official has an interest.
"(4) The governmental decision involves the imposition, repeal or
modification of any taxes or fees assessed or imposed on the real
property in which the official has an interest.
"(5) The governmental decision is to designate the survey area, to
select the project area, to adopt the preliminary plan, to form a
project area committee, to certify the environmental document, to
adopt the redevelopment plan, to add territory to the
redevelopment area, or to rescind or amend any of the above
decisions; and real property in which the official has an interest, or
any part of it is located within the boundaries (or the proposed
boundaries) of the redevelopment area.
"(6) The decision involves construction of, or improvements to,
streets, water, sewer, storm drainage or similar facilities, and the
real property in which the official has an interest will receive new
or improved services."
Because the real property owned by the business entities in which Commissioner Assemi has an
economic interest does not fall within any of the foregoing categories, it is deemed to be
indirectly involved.
Step 5. What is the applicable materiality standard?
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
governmental decision on a public official's economic interest is material. (Regulation
18700(a).)
Bustugss Et'trtnns
The applicable materiality standard for governmental decisions involving business
entities is found in Regulation 18705.1. Subdivision (c) sets forth materiality standards for
economic interests in business entities that are indirectly involved in a governmental decision,
including those that are sources of income. (See Regulation 18705.3(bX1). For relatively small
businesses, the financial effect of a governmental decision on the business entity is material if it
is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will increase/decrease the business
entity's annual gross revenues by $20,000 or more, result in the business entity
incurring/avoiding additional expenses or reducing/eliminating existing "*p"ni", by $5,000 or
more in a fiscal year or increase/decrease the value of the business entity's assets/liibilities by
$20,000 or more. (Regulation 18705.1(c)(4).) Based on the size of the business entities, as stated
in your letter, this is the applicable materiality standard.
Rset Pnopnnry
The financial effect of a governmental decision on real property that is indirectly
involved in the governmental decision is presumed not to be material. This presumption may be
rebutted by proof that there are specific circumstances regarding the governmental decision, its
financial effect, and the nature of the real property which make it reaionably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect on the real property in which tie public official has
an interest. Examples of such specific circumstances include the developmenipotential or
income producing potential of the real property, the use of the real property and the character of
the neighborhood, including substantial effects on traffic, view, privacy, intensity of use, noise
levels and air emissions. (Regularion 18705.2(b)(1).)
Step 6. Is it reasonably foreseeable that the financial effect of the Commission's decisions
on Commissioner Assemi's economic interests will meet the applicable materiality
standard?
An effect is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if the effect is "substantially
likely." (Regulation 18706; ln re Tlrcrner (1975) I FPPC Ops. 198.) 198.)198.) A financial
effect need not be a certainty to be considered reasonably foreseeable. On the other hand, if an
effect is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonabry foreseeable.
Bustttss ENrtrtos:
You have indicated that the CTC's decision will not affect any of the expenses of the
relevant businesses for a fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 or more. You have also stated that
"it is not practical to determine whether the proposed decision will have cmy financial effect on
those business entities and even more difficult to quantify that effect, if any." the decision on the
gross revenues/liabilities of the businesses would be speculative. Under these circumstances,
there is a possibility that the CTC's decision may meet the materiality thresholds set forth in
Regulation I 8705. I (c). However, as stated above, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable. The Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providing advice. (7,i
re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 72.) The determination of whether it is reaionably foreseeable
that the CTC's decision will affect the gross revenues of the business entities by $10,000 or
more, or the assets/liabilities by $5,000 or more, is necessarily a factual issue that is ultimately
for Commissioner Assemi to decide.
Roet Pnopmry:
La Ventana Property: The La Ventana property is located approximately 600 feet from
the proposed interchange and has been subdivided, improved and niirly all lots have been sold.
The lone exceptions are a proposed multi-family parcel and a proposed commercial parcel.
These parcels have received the necessary zoning and other basic entitlements.
Regulation 18705.2(b)(l) provides that the presumption of non-materiality may be
rebutted by proof that there are specific circumstances regarding the governmentil decision, its
financial effect, and the nature of the real property that make itieasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect on the real property. The location of the La
Ventana property, just 600 feet from the proposed interchange, and the nature of the parcel that is
zoned for commercial use are "specific circumstances" that iebut the presumption. denerally, it
can be expected that increased access to a commercial property by its proximity to a freeway
interchange would enhance the value of the property or increase iis iniome-producing pot"rrtiul.
Under these circumstances, Commissioner Assemi may not make, participate in making, or
attempt to influence governmental decisions regarding the proposed interihange.
Garfield Avenue Property: This property is located approximately 4,600 feet from the
proposed interchange. The Garfield Avenue property has secured zoning approvals and is the
subject of a vesting tentative map. The vesting tentative map establistres ttre conditions of
approval for each further phase of the subdivision. Because we have determined that
Commissioner Assemi may not participate in the govemmental decision regarding the proposed
interchange, we need not reach the question of whether the presumption of non-materiatity is
rebutted with respect to the Garfield Avenue property.
Westlake Property: The Westlake property is located approximately 1.7 miles from the
proposed interchange. A tentative map covering this property has been submitted and the
environmental document is being prepared. [t is expected that the property will be annexed into
the City of Fresno. [t is anticipated the proposed interchange will lielp relieve projected traffic
congestion in the immediate area, including the Westlake Property. Ii is also antiiipated that
residents as far west as the town of Kerman, located approximately l5 miles from tie proposecl
interchange, will benetlt from improved access to the greater Fresno community. Becansi we
have determined that Commissioner Assemi may not participate in the govemmental decision
regarding the proposed interchange, we need not reach the question-of whether the presumption
of non-materiality is rebutted with respect to the westlake prope.ty.2
Step 7: The "public generally" exception.
Even if a material financial effect on a public official's economic interest is reasonably
foreseeable, he or she still may not be disqualified if the financial effect of the govemmental
decision on the public official's economic interest is indistinguishable from its Lff""t on the
public generally. (Section 87103; Regulations 18700(b)(7) and 1g707(a).) Because
Commissioner Assemi has substantial business interests and property holdings, it is extremely
unlikely that his interests will be affected in substantially the same manner as the public
generally.
Step 8: The'olegally required participation, exception.
You have not presented any facts indicating that this exception is applicable to
Commissioner Assemi's situation.

QUESTION: As a member of the Califomia Transportation Commission (the "CTC"), may
Commissioner Darius Assemi make, participate in making, or otherwise use his official position
to influence the CTC's decisions relating to the constmction of a highway interchange in Fresno
Cotmty, if he has investments in business entities that own real property in the county?

CONCLUSION: Commissioner Assemi may not make, participate in making or Llse his official position to
influence the CTC's decisions. It is reasonably foreseeable that the CTC's decisions will have a
material financial effect on his real property because the interchange would increase access to the
property and would, therefore, increase the value of the property and its income producing
potential.

embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 September 16, 2010 John Bezmalinovic Granville Homes, Inc. 1396 West Herndon Avenue, Suite 101 Fresno, California 93711 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-10-137 Dear Mr. Bezmalinovic: This letter is in response to your request for advice, on behalf of Darius Assemi, regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090. QUESTION As a member of the California Transportation Commission (the "CTC"), may Commissioner Darius Assemi make, participate in making, or otherwise use his official position to influence the CTC's decisions relating to the construction of a highway interchange in Fresno County, if he has investments in business entities that own real property in the county? CONCLUSION Commissioner Assemi may not make, participate in making or use his official position to influence the CTC's decisions. It is reasonably foreseeable that the CTC's decisions will have a material financial effect on his real property because the interchange would increase access to the property and would, therefore, increase the value of the property and its income producing potential. ¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No. A-10-137 Page No. 2 FACTS Darius Assemi serves as a commissioner of the CTC. The CTC is responsible for: • Adopting a five-year State Transportation Improvement Program ("STIP") and approving the biennial four-year State Highway Operation and Protection Program ("SHOPP"). • Adopting a biennial five-year fund estimate of state and federal funds expected to be available for the STIP and SHOPP. • Allocating state funds for capital projects, consistent with the STIP and SHOPP. • Allocating state funds for capital grants from the Aeronautics Account and from the Environmental Enhancement and Mitigation Program Fund. • Adopting guidelines for the development of the STIP and for the development of regional transportation plans. • Advising and assisting the Secretary of Business, Transportation and Housing Agency and the Legislature in formulating and evaluating state policies and plans for transportation programs in the state. • Adopting and submitting to the Legislature an annual report summarizing major policies and decisions adopted by the CTC and identifying timely and relevant transportation issues, including a discussion of any significant upcoming transportation issues anticipated to be of concern to the public and the legislature. It is anticipated that the CTC will be asked to approve a new connection to
